serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw Chinese chestnuts is approximately 80 g (about 10 medium chestnuts), providing roughly 179 kcal and 39 g of carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Chestnut, Raw — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central (NDB 12098)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09030025, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, dietary fibre, sugar, vitamin C, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, water) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170569 'Chestnuts, Chinese, raw' as the closest approved-source equivalent for fields not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 54 (low) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for chestnuts (boiled/raw). Raw chestnuts have a reported GI of approximately 54 (glucose reference). Glycemic load per 80 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (54 × 39.1) / 100 ≈ 21.1. Available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate − dietary fibre = 49.07 − 8.1 = 40.97 g/100g; per 80 g serving ≈ 32.8 g net carb; GL = (54 × 32.8) / 100 ≈ 17.7. Note: using total carbohydrate basis per serving (49.07 × 0.8 = 39.3 g), GL = (54 × 39.3) / 100 ≈ 21.2, rounded to 21.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100g) and high potassium (447 mg/100g) yielding an exceptionally favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 149:1
hypertension.guidance: Raw Chinese chestnuts are suitable for individuals with hypertension due to negligible sodium and high potassium content; avoid salted or candied chestnut preparations and monitor portion size given the relatively high carbohydrate load of 49 g/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~54) combined with high dietary fibre (8.1 g/100g) moderates postprandial glucose response, but total carbohydrate is high at 49 g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may include raw Chinese chestnuts in a controlled portion of 50–80 g per serving, preferring boiling or steaming over roasting with added sugar, and should monitor postprandial blood glucose as GI can vary with preparation method.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.11 g/100g), negligible saturated fat (0.21 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (8.1 g/100g), and high potassium (447 mg/100g) collectively support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw Chinese chestnuts are a heart-friendly nut choice with very low saturated fat and zero cholesterol; the high fibre and potassium content support LDL reduction and blood pressure management, making them suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease when consumed in appropriate portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (~43 mg/100g) as an antioxidant and high dietary fibre (8.1 g/100g) from a whole-food source; negligible saturated fat (0.21 g/100g) and no trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chinese chestnuts provide anti-inflammatory micronutrients including vitamin C and dietary fibre; avoid candied or salted preparations as added sugar and sodium may exacerbate RA-related inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese chestnut (Castanea mollissima), consumed raw, is a starchy tree nut native to China and widely used across East and Southeast Asian cuisines. Raw chestnuts have a dark brown outer shell encasing a firm, cream-coloured kernel with a mildly sweet, nutty flavour and a dense, starchy texture that softens considerably upon cooking. Unlike most nuts, chestnuts are very low in fat (1.11 g/100g) and high in complex carbohydrates (49.07 g/100g), making them nutritionally closer to a starchy food than a conventional nut. They provide 224 kcal per 100g, 4.2 g protein, and a notable 8.1 g dietary fibre per 100g. Key micronutrients include potassium (447 mg/100g), vitamin C (~43 mg/100g), folate (~62 mcg DFE/100g), iron (1.41 mg/100g), and calcium (18 mg/100g). Sodium is negligible at 3 mg/100g. The glycemic index of raw chestnuts is approximately 54 (low GI), and the high fibre content moderates postprandial glucose absorption. Chestnuts are suitable for individuals with hypertension due to their exceptional potassium-to-sodium ratio. For Type 2 Diabetes management, portion control is essential given the high total carbohydrate content. Cardiovascular benefits arise from the very low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high fibre profile. In Chinese culinary tradition, chestnuts are used in braised dishes, stir-fries, soups, and as a roasted street snack. They are naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in raw chestnuts?
Is chestnut a nut or a starch?
Can I eat chestnuts if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09030025, confidence 0.95, reviewed status). Secondary nutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170569 for Chinese chestnut raw, an exact food match. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed international GI tables.
notes: cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central — chestnuts are a plant food and naturally contain no cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw chestnuts. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for raw chestnuts. Secondary nutrient values (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, B-vitamins, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170569 'Chestnuts, Chinese, raw' as the closest approved-source equivalent for fields not reported by HPB. GL calculation basis: GI 54 × (49.07 g carbohydrate per 100g × 0.80 serving fraction) / 100 = 54 × 39.3 / 100 ≈ 21.2, reported as 21.1. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03301446 and FoodEx2 A00LC are defensible matches for raw chestnut; SNOMED CT 256329004 is the substance-level code for chestnut. No Singaporean multilingual names (Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are required as this food is classified as Chinese cuisine, not a Singaporean dish; Chinese-script local names are provided.
